section.title: Introduction
section.para: The opennlp-geo extension turns place names into places on the map. Where the name finder tells an application that a span of text is a location, geographic resolution tells it which location: it takes the mention, looks it up in a gazetteer of known places, chooses among the candidates that share the name, and returns the chosen record with its coordinates. A mention of "Springfield" resolves to one of the many Springfields; a document that also mentions "Illinois" pushes the choice toward the one in Illinois.

The extension is built around two small interfaces. A Gazetteer is a lookup over a body of place records. A Geocoder chooses among the candidates a gazetteer returns for the mentions in a piece of text. A document annotator ties the two into a pipeline, reading the entity layer produced by the name finder and adding a layer of resolved locations. Later sections of this chapter add an administrative hierarchy over the resolved places and a similarity measure between places.

The extension bundles a small worldwide gazetteer so it works out of the box, and it reads the large open gazetteer formats that an application supplies itself. Consistent with the rest of the library, the large data sets are never packaged: the application downloads the source it wants and points the loader at it, accepting that source's license in doing so.

section.title: Gazetteers
section.para: The attribute map is how a record carries identifiers that do not belong to the core fields, for example a Wikidata id or a census geoid. The keys follow the ATTRIBUTE_KEY_* constants published on GazetteerEntry, so an application reads an attribute by the same constant a loader wrote it with. The administrative hierarchy later in this chapter joins on the ATTRIBUTE_KEY_WHOSONFIRST attribute in exactly this way.

section.title: Geocoding
Geocoding in a document pipeline
section.para: By default the annotator treats every entity as a candidate location. A second constructor takes the set of entity types to geocode, so a pipeline whose name finder emits several entity types can restrict geocoding to the location type it uses. new GeocodeAnnotator(geocoder, Set.of("location")); The locations layer keeps the original mention spans, so a resolved place lines up with the entity it came from and with every other layer in the document.
